what_it_does: Defines the frozen P0.3 format for a machine-checked, embedding-ready UI contract corpus that a UI-building AI consumes instead of reading the repo's code or disposable harness UI. It specifies directory layout, entry templates, operation specs, status/liveness/verification semantics, exposure registry rules, validation rules, coverage mapping, a navigation protocol, code-side build obligations, and a ledger of dialectic resolutions.
observations: Declares the format frozen at P0.3: entry content updates in place forever, while format changes require a recorded decision edited into the body with a changelog line.
Adjudicates a three-lens dialectic and adopts a resource-oriented spine, grafting selected rules from the event-sourced and capability-verbs lenses.
Uses a one-file-per-resource-noun layout under resources/, plus journeys/, atlas/, generated indexes, coverage artifacts, and tooling.
Mandates a uniform verb vocabulary: list, get, create, update, delete, act, watch, search; ad-hoc verbs are remodeled as resources or named acts.
Treats the contract:op fence as the single machine truth; prose and frontmatter are checked against it, never trusted over it.
Defines a status lifecycle (planned, building, live, broken, retired) where live flips and broken demotions are harness-driven, not hand-edited.
Requires verification per consequence branch and per binding, so one exercised branch cannot green-paint siblings.
Uses generated exposure.json for binding exposure values; inline exposure values are forbidden.
Lists 26 validation rules (V1–V26) split into static checks and reality-join checks that fail loud if the live system is unreachable.
Defines a two-layer coverage map: contracted claims versus demonstrated proof from blind-author-tagged task verdicts at affordance grain.
Imposes explicit code-side build obligations (structured route registries, OPS constants, EVENT_TYPES registry, uniform error envelope) so machine checks have real ground truth.
Includes a fully worked operation example for session.post covering request/response schemas, errors, consequences, synthetic examples, and verification branches.
Ends with a dialectic resolutions ledger mapping every critique finding to a spec section or repair.
standouts: The op template includes a complete worked example for session.post, making the abstract format concrete.
Broken status is written by the checker on evidence rot or reality mismatch, not by humans.
Coverage credit flows from blind task verdicts, not corpus self-tags, to prevent affordance-level gaming.
The format explicitly imposes build-side registries and conventions so validation has ground truth to compare against.
conventions: Markdown frontmatter plus fenced code blocks tagged contract:op, contract:schema, contract:error, contract:example, and contract:fsm for machine-readable artifacts.
Generated files are overwritten by tools; hand-edits fail validation.
Canonical documents are updated in place with no versioned filenames.
Every H2 section opens with a bold, distinct one-sentence restatement.
Uniform error envelope across HTTP, MCP, and CLI bindings.
Address schemes are owned by resource entries and checked for dereferenceability.
concerns: The file is very long and mixes normative spec, worked example, validation catalog, coverage mechanism, build obligations, and critique ledger, which may hinder chunk-level retrieval.
Many rules depend on companion files (README.md, CONVENTIONS.md, TRANSPORTS.md, atlas files, tools) whose contents are not shown here, so the file is not self-contained.
Heavy reliance on future harness and tooling behavior that is described but not implemented in this file.
Dated observed-repo assertions (e.g., 2026-06-11) will stale unless continuously regenerated.
notes: A dense, frozen P0.3 spec that tries to make a UI contract corpus machine-checkable, navigable, and honest about build status by binding format rules to generated registries and harness-driven verification.
questions: What are the exact contents and schema of the 35 FEATURE-ATLAS.md classes and affordances referenced here?
How are the uniform verb set and named-act registry defined in CONVENTIONS.md?
What is the precise parsing grammar for contract:op and contract:schema fences beyond the session.post example?
How does the harness safely replay captured evidence for mutating operations without side effects?
Which copy is authoritative when this build-prep file and the corpus copy diverge?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to define the contract format; the actual content does that plus adjudication rationale, build obligations, coverage mechanism, and a critique ledger, so it is broader than a pure format spec but aligned with its stated purpose.
apparent_themes: Single source of truth via opspec fences and generated registries
No silent gaps — exclusions must be explicit and reasoned
Event-sourced proof shape for writes via watch cursors and correlation keys
intention_signals: The file is trying to create a durable, auditable contract surface between a complex fabric system and UI-building agents, using a resource-oriented model, generated artifacts, and continuous validation to prevent drift.
novelty: Treats the contract corpus as the build's second product and the consumed artifact for AI; ties status/verification to harness-driven evidence replay with automatic demotion to broken; separates contracted claims from demonstrated blind-task verdicts in coverage; preserves design rationale in a dialectic resolutions ledger.
contribution: Provides a concrete schema and lifecycle for resource-oriented UI contracts, including directory layout, entry and operation templates, validation rules, coverage mapping, exposure semantics, and build-side registry obligations, that a fused system could keep as a contract-format substrate.
summary_for_embedding: This file is the frozen P0.3 specification for a machine-readable UI contract corpus, defining directory layout, resource entry templates, operation specs, status/verification lifecycle, exposure registries, validation rules, coverage mapping, navigation protocol, and code-side build obligations.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to establish a durable, machine-checked contract format that UI-building agents consume instead of reading code or disposable UI harnesses.
